topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion in the quiet hum of my afternoon. It was a post from Mr. Harrison, my AP History teacher. My breath hitched, a sudden tightness in my chest. I stared at the screen, my thumb hovering over the 'view profile' button, a strange mixture of confusion and a deep, unsettling unease swirling inside me. He had posted a picture of a book he was reading, captioned with a philosophical quote. It was innocuous, really, but seeing it there, linked directly to my own account, made my stomach clench. I quickly minimized the app, my hands trembling slightly as I tried to steady my breathing. The classroom felt suddenly too bright, the air too thick. I felt a prickle of alarm crawl up my neck, wondering what that meant, what connection existed between us now.

[story 2]

I was scrolling through my feed, trying to distract myself from the looming essay due tomorrow, when I saw it. A picture of a campus event, clearly taken by Mr. Harrison’s phone, and my name was tagged in the background. A wave of heat rushed over my face, making my cheeks flush. I dropped my phone onto my desk with a soft thud. My jaw felt tight, and I could feel the rapid drumming of my pulse against my ribs. Why was he looking at my activity? It felt invasive, like being watched under a microscope. I quickly locked my screen, my fingers fumbling with the passcode. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming a warning signal. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing distance from the glowing rectangle that had just introduced a profound, uncomfortable shift in my perception of my teacher.

[story 3]

It happened during a late-night study session. I was reviewing notes, feeling the familiar weight of anxiety settle in my shoulders. Then, a notification chimed. It was Mr. Harrison. A short video clip—a casual, smiling clip from a weekend hike. My heart gave a painful lurch. I didn't move for a full minute, just staring at the screen, trying to process the unexpected intimacy of the shared digital space. A strange, hollow feeling settled in my core, a sense of being exposed in a way that felt deeply wrong. When I finally typed a reply—a simple, polite acknowledgment—my fingers felt clumsy and detached. The silence in my room amplified the sudden, sharp awareness that a line had been crossed, a boundary I hadn't even known existed, now undeniably blurred.
